# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kubernetes Deployment that pulls our application image from Docker Hub. Use the latest tag so we always get the newest version automatically. This is for our development environment where we want continuous updates without manual intervention.

Use platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent container security.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1  # Kubernetes API Version (apps) v1 used in this case, it's the version of our resource Deployment which we are creating here for development purposes only and not meant as a production environment setup. For real-world use cases you should replace with your actual api versions
kind: Deployment   # Kind is to specify what type object Kubernetes API will create (Deployments in this case) 
metadata:           # Metadata contains information about the deployment, such as name and namespace of our application. In real-world use cases you should replace with your actual metadata details like namespaces etc.. For example -> "name": "myapp", "namespace":"dev" or similar for development environments only  
  labels:           # Labels are used to select the pod(s) that will be managed by this deployment. In real-world use cases you should replace with your actual label details like app, env etc.. For example -> "labels": {"app": "myApp", "env":"dev"} or similar for development environments only
    name: myDeployment   # Name of the Deployments in Kubernetes. In real-world use cases you should replace with your actual names 
specs:              # Specification details about how to create and manage pod(s) that will be managed by this deployment (like number, type etc.) For example -> "replicas":3   or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
  selector:         # Selector helps Kubernetes know which pods to manage when it creates new ones (like labels etc.) For example -> "matchLabels": {"app":"myApp", "env" :'dev'} or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
    matchLabels:     # Match Labels are what selectors Kubernetes uses to find pod(s) that will be managed by this deployment (like labels etc.) For example -> {"app": "myApp", “env" :'dev'} or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
   template:         # Template describes how to create pods and containers (like Dockerfile etc.) For example -> spec/containers[0]/imagePullPolicy : "Always" or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
     metadata:       # Metadata contains information about this pod (like name, labels etc.) For example -> {"name":'myApp', 'labels': {'app":"dev"}} or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
     spec:           # Specification details about how to create pods and containers (like Dockerfile etc.) For example -> image : "myDockHubUser/app" or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
       container:    # Container describes what will be run inside this pod and how it should behave (like Dockerfile etc.) For example -> name : "myApp", imagePullPolicy:"Always" or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
         image: myDockHubUser/app   # Image specifies what Docker Hub user and repository to use when pulling images (like username / repo name). For example -> "myDockHubUser/app" or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
         imagePullPolicy: Always   # Image Pull Policy specifies how to pull images from Docker Hub (like IfNotPresent, Always etc.) For example -> "Always" or similar for development environments only. This is not meant as a production environment setup but used here just in the context of dev env
